coverage_notes: Direct match. Covers electron transfer mechanism, ion formation (cations/anions), crystal lattice structure, and the characteristic properties of ionic compounds (high melting point, brittle, conducts when dissolved/melted, soluble in water).

rendering_guide.section: Headed paragraph block with spacing above/below
rendering_guide.callout: Highlighted box. Styles: definition, key_idea, fun_fact, remember, mind_blow
rendering_guide.list: Styled list. Styles: icon_list, two_column, comparison, challenge, numbered_steps
rendering_guide.transition: Italic bridge text, lighter font weight

student_flow.step_2: READ Part 2 (core — how ionic bonds form, ions, electron transfer)
student_flow.step_5: READ Part 3 (crystal lattice + properties of ionic compounds)
student_flow.step_8: READ Part 4 (big picture + bridge to covalent_bonding)

parts.purpose: Hook — the dramatic reaction that creates table salt

blocks.body: Sodium is a soft, silvery metal that explodes when it touches water. Chlorine is a yellow-green toxic gas that was used as a chemical weapon in World War I. Put them together and you get — table salt. The white stuff on your dinner. Completely harmless.

Two of the most dangerous elements on the periodic table combine to form one of the safest and most essential substances for human life. The reaction is violent — bright flame, intense heat — but the product is something you eat every day.

What happened? Sodium handed its lone valence electron to chlorine. Both achieved full outer shells. And the resulting charged particles — one positive, one negative — locked together by electrostatic attraction. That's an ionic bond. And it's the first of the three bonding strategies we'll explore in depth.

blocks.title: Watch It Happen
blocks.body: If you can, search for a video of sodium reacting with chlorine gas. You'll see the sodium glow with an intense yellow-orange flame as it burns in the chlorine atmosphere. White smoke fills the container. When the reaction finishes, the inside is coated with white crystals — pure sodium chloride.

Now pick up your salt shaker. Those tiny white crystals are the product of that violent reaction. Each grain contains billions of sodium and chloride ions locked in a perfect alternating grid, held together by the same force that created that dramatic flame.

The violent reaction happens because both atoms desperately want stable electron configurations. Sodium can't wait to lose its electron. Chlorine can't wait to gain one. When they meet, the transfer happens so fast and releases so much energy that it produces flame and heat.
Sodium: explodes in water. Chlorine: literal poison gas. Together: goes great on french fries. Chemistry is wild.

The reaction between sodium and chlorine is so energetic it produces a bright yellow flame visible from across a lab. Two substances that could individually kill you combine to produce something you voluntarily eat multiple times a day. If that doesn't convince you chemistry is interesting, nothing will.

So what actually happened? Sodium said 'TAKE MY ELECTRON' and chlorine said 'GLADLY.' The electron changed hands, both atoms achieved full shells, and the resulting charged particles got stuck together. That's ionic bonding — the chemical equivalent of a very dramatic gift exchange.

parts.purpose: Core teaching — electron transfer, cation/anion formation, electrostatic attraction

blocks.title: The Three-Step Process
blocks.body: Every ionic bond forms through the same sequence: an atom loses electrons, another atom gains electrons, and the resulting charged ions attract each other.

items.label: Step 1: Metal Loses Electrons → Cation
items.body: Sodium (2-8-1) loses its 1 valence electron. It now has 11 protons (+) but only 10 electrons (−). Net charge: +1. Sodium becomes Na⁺, a cation (positive ion). Its electron configuration is now 2-8 — identical to neon. Full outer shell achieved.

items.label: Step 2: Nonmetal Gains Electrons → Anion
items.body: Chlorine (2-8-7) gains that electron. It now has 17 protons (+) but 18 electrons (−). Net charge: −1. Chlorine becomes Cl⁻, an anion (negative ion). Its configuration is now 2-8-8 — identical to argon. Full outer shell achieved.

items.label: Step 3: Opposite Charges Attract → Ionic Bond
items.body: Na⁺ (positive) and Cl⁻ (negative) are drawn together by electrostatic attraction — the same force that makes opposite magnet poles attract. This attraction IS the ionic bond. It's strong, directional, and holds the ions in a fixed arrangement.

blocks.description: Diagram showing: Na atom (2-8-1) → loses 1e⁻ → Na⁺ (2-8). Cl atom (2-8-7) → gains 1e⁻ → Cl⁻ (2-8-8). Arrow showing electron transfer. Then Na⁺ and Cl⁻ attracted together.

blocks.title: Cation vs Anion
blocks.body: Cation: a positive ion (lost electrons). Metals form cations. The 't' in cation looks like a '+' sign. Anion: a negative ion (gained electrons). Nonmetals form anions.

blocks.title: Beyond 1:1 — Predicting Ratios
blocks.body: Not all ionic compounds are 1:1. The ratio depends on how many electrons need to transfer to give both atoms full shells.

items.definition: Mg loses 2, each Cl gains 1 → 1 Mg needs 2 Cl
Al loses 3, O gains 2 → 2 Al (6 e⁻ total) = 3 O (6 e⁻ total)

blocks.title: The Pattern
blocks.body: The formula of an ionic compound is always the simplest ratio that balances total electrons lost with total electrons gained. No compound has a net charge — the positive and negative must cancel.

blocks.title: Build It With Lewis Dots
blocks.body: Draw sodium's Lewis dot (Na with 1 dot) and chlorine's (Cl with 7 dots — 3 pairs + 1 single). Now physically draw an arrow moving sodium's dot to chlorine's empty spot.
Sodium now has 0 outer dots — write Na⁺. Chlorine now has 8 dots (4 pairs) — write Cl⁻. Draw a bracket around the Cl with its 8 dots and label it with a minus sign. The transfer is complete.

blocks.title: Now Try Magnesium + Chlorine
blocks.body: Magnesium has 2 dots. Each chlorine has 7. Magnesium needs to lose 2 electrons. Each chlorine can only accept 1. So you need TWO chlorines. Draw Mg with 2 dots. Draw two Cl atoms, each with 7 dots. Move 1 dot from Mg to the first Cl. Move the other dot to the second Cl. Result: Mg²⁺ + 2Cl⁻ → MgCl₂.

The Lewis dot method makes ratios obvious. If magnesium has 2 electrons to give and each chlorine takes 1, you need 2 chlorines. If aluminum has 3 to give and oxygen takes 2, the lowest common multiple is 6: 2 aluminum (giving 6 total) and 3 oxygen (taking 6 total) → Al₂O₃.

blocks.title: Feel the Charge
blocks.body: Rub a balloon on your hair. The balloon steals electrons from your hair (becoming slightly negative) and your hair loses electrons (becoming slightly positive). Your hair stands up — attracted toward the negatively charged balloon. You just demonstrated the same electrostatic attraction that holds Na⁺ and Cl⁻ together. Ionic bonds work on the same principle, just at the atomic scale.

blocks.title: The Three-Act Drama of Ionic Bonding
blocks.body: Every ionic bond is a short play with three acts.

items.label: Act 1: The Donation
items.body: Metal atom, sick of holding onto valence electrons it doesn't need, YEETS them into space. Sodium: 'I don't even WANT this electron.' Loses 1 → becomes Na⁺. Magnesium: 'Take TWO.' Loses 2 → becomes Mg²⁺. These positive ions are called cations. Easy memory trick: the 't' in cation looks like a '+' sign.

items.label: Act 2: The Catch
items.body: Nonmetal atom, desperately needing electrons, CATCHES them. Chlorine: 'FINALLY, my octet is complete.' Gains 1 → becomes Cl⁻. Oxygen: 'I'll take two, thanks.' Gains 2 → becomes O²⁻. These negative ions are called anions. They're 'A Negative Ion.' AN-ion.

items.label: Act 3: The Attraction
items.body: Positive cation and negative anion are now electrostatically attracted. They snap together like opposite magnets. That attraction IS the bond. No sharing, no compromise — one gave, one took, and now they're stuck together by physics.

blocks.title: The Ratio Logic
blocks.body: Why is magnesium chloride MgCl₂ and not MgCl? Because Mg dumps 2 electrons but each Cl only catches 1. You need TWO catchers for ONE thrower. The formula isn't random — it's just electron accounting. Total given = total taken. Always.

Aluminum oxide is Al₂O₃ because aluminum throws 3 electrons each and oxygen catches 2 each. Least common multiple of 3 and 2 is 6. So you need 2 aluminums (throwing 6 total) and 3 oxygens (catching 6 total). It's literally just fraction math with explosions.

questions.question: When sodium (Na) becomes Na⁺, what happened?

options.A: It gained a proton, making it more positive
options.B: It lost 1 electron — now it has more protons than electrons, giving it a +1 charge
options.C: It gained an electron from chlorine
options.D: A neutron converted into a proton

questions.explanation: Sodium lost its 1 valence electron. It still has 11 protons (+11) but now only 10 electrons (−10). Net charge: +1. The number of protons never changes in chemical reactions — only electrons move.

questions.question: Magnesium (2 valence electrons) bonds with fluorine (7 valence electrons). Predict the formula.

options.B: MgF₂ — magnesium gives 2 electrons, each fluorine takes 1, so 2 fluorines needed
options.C: Mg₂F — two magnesiums for one fluorine
options.D: MgF₇ — one fluorine for each of magnesium's electrons plus fluorine's

questions.explanation: Mg has 2 electrons to give. F needs 1 each. So 1 Mg needs 2 F atoms to balance: Mg²⁺ + 2F⁻ → MgF₂. Total electrons given (2) = total electrons gained (1 + 1 = 2).

questions.question: Why do ionic bonds form specifically between metals and nonmetals?

options.A: Metals are heavier and fall toward nonmetals due to gravity
options.B: Metals have low electronegativity (willing to lose electrons) and nonmetals have high electronegativity (eager to gain) — the large EN difference drives electron transfer
options.C: Metals and nonmetals are on opposite sides of the table, so they attract magnetically
options.D: Only metals have electrons to give

questions.explanation: The large electronegativity gap between metals (low EN, loosely held electrons) and nonmetals (high EN, strongly attracting electrons) makes electron transfer energetically favorable. This is why ionic bonds form across the metal-nonmetal divide.

parts.purpose: Teach the crystal lattice structure and the distinctive properties of ionic compounds

blocks.title: Ionic Compounds Don't Form Molecules
blocks.body: Here's a crucial distinction: NaCl isn't a molecule. There's no single Na attached to a single Cl floating around independently. Instead, ionic compounds form massive three-dimensional grids called crystal lattices — every positive ion is surrounded by negative ions, and every negative ion is surrounded by positive ions, extending in all directions.

blocks.title: Crystal Lattice
blocks.body: A repeating 3D arrangement of alternating positive and negative ions. Each ion is attracted to ALL neighboring opposite ions, not just one partner. The formula NaCl simply describes the simplest ratio in the lattice — for every Na⁺, there's one Cl⁻.

blocks.description: 3D crystal lattice diagram showing alternating Na⁺ (small, blue) and Cl⁻ (large, green) ions in a cubic arrangement. Label showing each Na⁺ surrounded by 6 Cl⁻ and vice versa.

blocks.title: Properties That Follow From the Lattice
blocks.body: The crystal lattice structure explains every distinctive property of ionic compounds.

items.label: High Melting and Boiling Points
items.body: It takes enormous energy to break apart a lattice where every ion is attracted to multiple neighbors. NaCl melts at 801°C. MgO melts at 2,852°C. You need to overcome billions of electrostatic attractions simultaneously.

items.label: Hard but Brittle
items.body: The lattice is rigid — ions are locked in position. Hard to deform (hard), but if you hit it with enough force, layers of ions shift. When they shift, positive ions suddenly face positive ions — the repulsion shatters the crystal along clean planes.

items.label: Conduct Electricity When Dissolved or Melted (Not Solid)
items.body: In solid form, ions are locked in place and can't carry charge. Dissolve in water or melt, and the ions become free to move — they carry electrical charge through the liquid. This is why saltwater conducts electricity but dry salt doesn't.

items.label: Often Soluble in Water
items.body: Water molecules are polar (oxygen side is slightly negative, hydrogen side is slightly positive). They can surround individual ions, pulling them out of the lattice one by one. The negative end of water surrounds Na⁺, the positive end surrounds Cl⁻ — this is dissolving.

items.label: Form Crystalline Solids
items.body: Because the lattice is regular and repeating, ionic compounds form clean geometric crystals. Salt crystals are always cubic because the NaCl lattice is cubic. The crystal shape reflects the atomic arrangement.

blocks.title: Ionic Compound Fingerprint
blocks.body: High melting point + hard but brittle + conducts when dissolved/melted + crystalline solid + often dissolves in water = almost certainly ionic.

blocks.title: Test the Properties Yourself
blocks.body: Grab some table salt and verify these properties hands-on.

items.label: Crystal Structure
items.body: Look at salt under a magnifying glass (or zoom your phone camera). You'll see tiny cubes — that's the cubic NaCl lattice visible to the naked eye. Every crystal is a tiny cube because the atomic arrangement is cubic.

items.label: Hardness + Brittleness
items.body: Put a salt crystal on a hard surface and press a spoon down on it. It resists at first (hard), then suddenly shatters into smaller cubes (brittle). It doesn't bend or squish — it snaps along crystal planes.

items.label: Dissolving
items.body: Drop salt in warm water and stir. It vanishes — water molecules are pulling Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions out of the lattice one by one, surrounding each ion with a shell of water molecules.

items.label: Conductivity
items.body: If you have a battery and a small LED, test dry salt (doesn't conduct — LED stays off) vs saltwater (conducts — LED lights up). The dissolved ions carry the current.

blocks.body: Every property you just tested — cubic crystals, hard but brittle, dissolves in water, conducts only when dissolved — traces directly to the crystal lattice structure. The structure explains the behavior.

blocks.title: The Crystal Lattice: World's Most Organized Party
blocks.body: Ionic compounds don't form individual couples (molecules). They form massive group arrangements where every positive ion is surrounded by negatives and every negative is surrounded by positives. It's the most organized party in chemistry — everyone has assigned positions and nobody's allowed to leave.

blocks.title: The Properties Make Perfect Sense
blocks.body: Once you picture the lattice, every property clicks.

items.label: High Melting Point
items.body: To melt the lattice you have to break EVERY attraction simultaneously. NaCl doesn't melt until 801°C. That's an oven you don't own. The lattice is THAT stable.

items.label: Hard but Brittle
items.body: The lattice is rigid — ions don't budge. But smack it hard enough and layers shift. Positive meets positive. The entire plane REPELS simultaneously and the crystal shatters. It's like pushing two groups of same-pole magnets together — instant rejection.

items.label: Conducts Only When Dissolved
items.body: Solid salt: ions are frozen in place. Electricity says 'no mobile charges, no service.' Saltwater: ions are free agents swimming around. Electricity says 'welcome aboard.' This is why your phone works fine in rain but a salt pool would fry it.

items.label: Dissolves in Water
items.body: Water molecules are polar — slightly charged on each end. They surround individual ions like tiny magnets, ripping them out of the lattice one by one. It's a molecular extraction. Water is basically a solvent SWAT team.

blocks.body: Salt crystals are always cubes because the NaCl lattice is cubic. If you break a salt crystal, the pieces are also cubes. It's cubes all the way down. The geometry of the lattice IS the geometry of the crystal. Fractal salt.

questions.question: Why does solid NaCl NOT conduct electricity, but dissolved NaCl does?

options.A: Solid NaCl doesn't contain ions — ions only form when salt dissolves
options.B: In solid NaCl, ions are locked in the crystal lattice and can't move. When dissolved, ions are free to move and carry charge
options.C: Water creates electricity that the salt conducts
options.D: The NaCl molecules break apart into atoms when dissolved

questions.explanation: Solid NaCl is already made of Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions — but they're locked in fixed positions in the crystal lattice. No movement = no charge flow. When dissolved, water separates the ions and they're free to move through the solution, carrying electrical current.

questions.question: You hit a salt crystal with a hammer and it shatters into smaller cubes. Why does it shatter rather than bend?

options.A: Salt is a gas that was temporarily frozen into solid form
options.B: The hammer's force shifts ion layers so that like charges face each other, causing violent repulsion that cracks the crystal along clean planes
options.C: Salt crystals contain microscopic air bubbles that burst under pressure
options.D: The water inside the crystal evaporates suddenly

questions.explanation: In the lattice, positive and negative ions alternate. A strong force shifts one layer relative to the next, aligning positive ions with positive ions (and negative with negative). The sudden repulsion between like charges splits the crystal along that plane — producing clean, flat fracture surfaces.

questions.question: NaCl melts at 801°C while sugar (a covalent compound) melts at 186°C. Why is the ionic compound's melting point so much higher?

options.A: NaCl weighs more than sugar molecules
options.B: Ionic lattices have strong electrostatic attractions between billions of ions in all directions, requiring enormous energy to disrupt
options.C: Sugar molecules are smaller so they melt faster
options.D: Salt absorbs heat better than sugar

questions.explanation: In NaCl, every ion is attracted to ALL its neighbors in a 3D lattice. Melting requires breaking billions of strong electrostatic attractions simultaneously. Sugar molecules are held together by much weaker forces between individual molecules, requiring far less energy to separate.

parts.purpose: Real-world examples, common ionic compounds, bridge to covalent_bonding

blocks.title: Ionic Compounds Are Everywhere
blocks.body: Now that you understand how ionic bonds work, you can spot them everywhere in daily life.

items.label: Sodium Chloride — NaCl
items.body: Table salt. Essential for nerve function and fluid balance. The most familiar ionic compound. Na⁺ and Cl⁻ in a cubic lattice.

items.label: Calcium Carbonate — CaCO₃
items.body: Chalk, limestone, marble, antacid tablets (Tums), eggshells, seashells. Ca²⁺ ions bonded with CO₃²⁻ ions. The same compound builds cliffs and settles your stomach.

items.label: Sodium Bicarbonate — NaHCO₃
items.body: Baking soda. Na⁺ and HCO₃⁻ ions. Used in baking (reacts with acid to produce CO₂ bubbles), cleaning, and as an antacid.

items.label: Iron Oxide — Fe₂O₃
items.body: Rust. Fe³⁺ and O²⁻ ions. Forms when iron reacts with oxygen and water over time. Also the red pigment in red sandstone and Mars's surface.

items.label: Calcium Phosphate — Ca₃(PO₄)₂
items.body: The primary mineral in your bones and teeth. Ca²⁺ and PO₄³⁻ ions arranged in a lattice that gives your skeleton its rigidity.

blocks.title: Spotting Ionic Compounds
blocks.body: Quick identification: if a compound contains a metal + nonmetal, it's almost certainly ionic. If it's a hard crystalline solid that dissolves in water and the solution conducts — ionic. The periodic table position of the elements tells you the bond type.

Ionic bonding handles the metal-nonmetal pairs. But what happens when two nonmetals meet? Neither one will give up electrons — both have high electronegativity. The solution: instead of transferring electrons, they SHARE them. That's covalent bonding — the second great strategy — and it creates molecules, not lattices. Everything from water to DNA to plastic is covalent. That's next.

blocks.title: Ionic Compound Scavenger Hunt
blocks.body: Find these ionic compounds in your home. They're more common than you'd think.

items.label: In your kitchen
items.hints: Table salt (NaCl), baking soda (NaHCO₃), calcium-fortified orange juice (added Ca²⁺), hard water deposits on faucets (CaCO₃).

items.label: In your bathroom
items.hints: Fluoride in toothpaste (NaF or SnF₂), Epsom salts for baths (MgSO₄), antacid tablets (CaCO₃ or Mg(OH)₂).

items.label: On your body
items.hints: Calcium phosphate in your bones and teeth (Ca₃(PO₄)₂), sodium and potassium ions in every nerve signal (Na⁺/K⁺), iron in your blood hemoglobin (Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺).

items.label: Outside
items.hints: Rust on metal (Fe₂O₃), road salt (NaCl or CaCl₂), limestone buildings (CaCO₃), concrete (contains calcium silicates).

blocks.body: Ionic compounds are the minerals of the world — the hard, crystalline, high-melting substances that build structures, support bones, and carry electrical signals. They're the structural engineers of chemistry.

But not everything is hard and crystalline. Water is a liquid. Plastic is flexible. Gas is invisible. These materials need a different kind of bond — one that creates discrete molecules instead of infinite lattices. Enter covalent bonding: electron sharing between nonmetals.

blocks.title: Ionic Compounds: The Greatest Hits
blocks.body: Now that you know what ionic bonding is, let's appreciate how many ionic compounds are part of your daily life without you ever noticing.

items.label: NaCl — Table Salt
items.body: The poster child of ionic bonding. On your food, in your blood, in the ocean. Humans have fought wars over this compound. It's literally just Na⁺ and Cl⁻ cubes.

items.label: CaCO₃ — Literally Everything
items.body: Chalk, limestone, marble, eggshells, seashells, antacid tablets, pearls. This one compound builds the White Cliffs of Dover AND settles your upset stomach. Versatility king.

items.label: Fe₂O₃ — Rust
items.body: The bane of every car owner. Also the reason Mars is red. Iron gave its electrons to oxygen and now your bike frame is slowly returning to the earth.

items.label: Ca₃(PO₄)₂ — Your Skeleton
items.body: Your bones are an ionic crystal lattice. You are literally walking around on crystal pillars. You are part mineral. Let that sink in.

blocks.body: Quick way to spot ionic compounds: does it contain a metal? Is it crystalline? Does it dissolve in water and make the water conduct electricity? Congratulations, it's ionic. You can now walk through a grocery store and mentally classify half the ingredients. Your friends will not find this as exciting as you do.

Ionic bonding covers metal + nonmetal. But what about nonmetal + nonmetal? Neither atom will give up its electrons — both are too greedy. The solution? They SHARE. Like an unhappy compromise that somehow creates every molecule in your body. That's covalent bonding. Next lesson.

questions.question: You find a white crystalline powder in your kitchen. It dissolves in water and the solution conducts electricity. What bond type is it most likely?

options.A: Covalent — most kitchen chemicals are covalent
options.C: Ionic — crystalline + dissolves + conducts when dissolved is the classic ionic fingerprint
options.D: No bonds — it's probably an element

questions.explanation: Crystalline solid + water-soluble + conducts when dissolved = ionic compound. The crystal structure comes from the lattice, dissolution releases ions, and those free ions carry electrical charge. This matches salt, baking soda, or many other ionic kitchen compounds.

questions.question: Calcium phosphate (Ca₃(PO₄)₂) makes bones rigid. Collagen (covalent protein) makes bones flexible. Why does bone need BOTH?

options.A: The body couldn't decide which to use
options.B: Ionic lattice provides hardness and compression strength, while covalent chains provide flexibility and resistance to snapping — together they create a material that's both strong and resilient
options.C: Calcium phosphate is temporary and collagen is permanent
options.D: They cancel each other's weaknesses randomly

questions.explanation: Pure ionic crystal (like chalk) would be hard but brittle — bones would shatter. Pure covalent protein would be flexible but soft — bones couldn't support weight. The combination gives bone its unique strength: rigid enough to bear weight, flexible enough to absorb impact without snapping.
